originalTitle: Ebola risk raised to 'very high' in DR Congo
title: Let's Learn About Staying Healthy in DR Congo!
content:
Hello, My Amazing Friends!

Hi everyone! I'm your friendly news reporter, here to share important news from our amazing continent, Africa. Today, we're going to talk about a country called the Democratic Republic of Congo, or DR Congo for short. It's a big country, just like a neighbor in our African family.

Meet Our Neighbor: DR Congo!

DR Congo is a beautiful country in the middle of Africa. It has big rivers, tall trees, and lots of lovely people. Some of our friends there are facing a challenge, and we want to understand it and send them our good wishes. Did you know? DR Congo is home to many different kinds of animals, like gorillas and okapis! It's a land full of wonders.

What is This Sickness Called Ebola?

Sometimes, a very tiny germ can make people sick. One of these sicknesses is called Ebola. It's not a monster or a scary ghost, just a germ that can make people feel very unwell. Ebola gives people a fever, like when you feel very hot. It can also make their tummy hurt and make them feel very weak. But don't worry, grown-ups and doctors know how to help people who get Ebola. Did you know? The sickness 'Ebola' is named after a river in DR Congo where it was first found many, many years ago. It’s like naming a pet after where you found it!

How Does Ebola Spread?

Ebola is not like a cold or cough that spreads when someone sneezes in the air. That's good news! Instead, Ebola spreads when people touch someone who is very sick with Ebola, or touch their blood or other body fluids. It can also spread if you touch things that a very sick person has used, like their clothes. This is why washing our hands is super important! Did you know? Washing your hands with soap and water for 20 seconds is like singing 'Happy Birthday' twice! It's a super shield against germs!

What Does "Risk" Mean?

When we say "risk," it means the chance of something happening. Imagine you want to climb a tree. There's a "risk" you might fall, but you can be careful to make that risk small. When it comes to sickness, "risk" means the chance of someone getting sick. We talk about "very high risk," "high risk," and "low risk." "Very high risk" means there's a big chance. "High risk" means there's a good chance. "Low risk" means there's a very small chance.

The "Very High" Risk in One Place

Our health helpers, who are like health detectives for the whole world, say the chance of getting Ebola is "very high" in just one small part of DR Congo. This means people there need lots of help and care right now. They are working hard to stop the sickness.

The "High" Risk Nearby

For countries very close to that small part of DR Congo, the chance is "high." This means these neighbor countries need to be extra careful and prepared. They are making sure their doctors and nurses are ready to help if anyone gets sick.

Good News for the Rest of Us!

Now, here's some very important good news! For most of the world, and for most of Africa, the chance of getting Ebola is "low." That means a very, very small chance. It is not spreading all over the world. This is because our health helpers and brave doctors and nurses know what to do. They are like superheroes fighting germs! Did you know? Health workers wear special suits, like space suits, to protect themselves when they are helping very sick people. They are very brave!

Who Are Our Health Superheroes?

There's a special team of health helpers called the UN health agency. They work for the United Nations, which is like a big club of countries helping each other. These health helpers are like the captains of our health team. They work with doctors and nurses in DR Congo and nearby countries. They teach people how to stay safe and how to care for those who are sick. They bring medicines and special equipment. Did you know? Many doctors and nurses from all over the world go to help people in places like DR Congo. They are truly amazing helpers!

How Can We Stay Safe and Healthy?

Even though the risk is low for most of us, it's always good to remember how to stay healthy. These rules are good for fighting all kinds of germs, not just Ebola: 1. **Wash Your Hands Often!** Use soap and water, and wash for a long time, especially after playing, before eating, and after using the toilet. 2. **Don't Touch Sick People:** If someone is very sick, it's best to keep a little distance. Let grown-ups take care of them. 3. **Tell a Grown-Up:** If you feel hot, or your tummy hurts, or you just don't feel well, tell a parent or teacher right away. They will know what to do. 4. **Listen to Your Grown-Ups:** Your parents, teachers, and guardians want to keep you safe and healthy. Always listen to their advice. Did you know? Your hands can carry many germs without you even seeing them! That's why handwashing is like magic!

Let's Be Kind and Strong!

It's important to remember that the people in DR Congo are our friends and neighbors. We can send them our good thoughts and wishes. We can also do our part by staying healthy and following good hygiene rules. Together, by being smart and careful, we can help keep ourselves and our communities safe. Keep smiling, keep washing those hands, and keep learning! You are all amazing!
summary: The chance of people getting sick with Ebola is 'very high' in a small part of DR Congo, 'high' in nearby areas, but 'low' for most of the world, thanks to health helpers who know how to keep people safe.
whyMatters: This is important because we want our friends in DR Congo to be safe and healthy, and we learn how to protect ourselves and others from sickness by washing hands and listening to grown-ups.

originalTitle: UK scientists developing Ebola vaccine that could be ready for trials in months
title: UK scientists developing Ebola vaccine that could be ready for trials in months
content: The rare species of Ebola involved - known as Bundibugyo - kills around a third of those infected and has no proven vaccine yet.

originalTitle: Israeli blockade traps would-be Hajj pilgrim in Gaza
title: Grandma Najia's Big Dream Trip!
content:
Hello, amazing explorers! It's your friend here, ready to share a special story from our big, beautiful world! Today, we're going to learn about a kind grandma named Najia and her super important dream.

Najia's Big Dream Trip!

Our story today is about a very special grandma. Her name is Najia Abu Lehia. She is 64 years old! That's quite a lot of birthdays, isn't it? Najia lives in a place called Gaza. Gaza is a small area of land. It is next to the big blue sea. Many friendly families live there. Najia has a very big dream. She wants to go on a very special trip. This trip is called Hajj.

What is Hajj?

Hajj is a super important journey for people who are Muslim. It's like a special pilgrimage. A pilgrimage is a trip to a holy place. Muslims believe this trip is a very important part of their faith. It shows their love for God. They travel to a holy city called Mecca. Mecca is in a country called Saudi Arabia. It's a very long way from Gaza! Did you know? Hajj is one of the five most important things in Islam! It's like having five big rules to live by. When people go on Hajj, they pray a lot. They meet other Muslims from all over the world. It's a time for thinking about peace and kindness. Many people save money for many years to go on this trip. It is a trip they remember forever. Najia has wanted to go for a very long time.

Najia and Her Husband's Wish

Najia doesn't want to go alone. She wants to go with her dear husband. They have dreamed about this trip together for many, many years. Imagine wanting to do something special with your best friend! That's how Najia feels about going with her husband. They have planned and wished. They have prayed and hoped. Every year, they hope it will be their turn. Did you know? Sometimes, families travel together for Hajj. It can be a wonderful family adventure! Najia has been waiting for a very long time. She has tried to go many times. But something has made it very, very difficult. It's like a big invisible wall that makes it hard to leave her home.

Living in Gaza

Gaza is a unique place. It is a small strip of land. It is very crowded. Many people live there. Gaza has many challenges. Sometimes, it is hard for people to travel in and out of Gaza. There are strict rules. These rules are sometimes called a "blockade." It means that leaving or entering is not easy. It's like having a big fence all around your neighborhood. You need special permission to go outside. And sometimes, that permission is very hard to get. Did you know? The Gaza Strip is only about 41 kilometers long and 10 kilometers wide! That's like a very long, narrow park. These rules are there because of many different reasons. It makes life tricky for many people. Imagine if you wanted to visit your grandma in another town, but you needed to ask many people for special stamps and papers just to leave your street! That's a bit what it's like for Najia.

The Difficult Wait

Najia has tried to get permission to go on Hajj many times. Every time she tries, she hopes and waits. But so far, she has not been able to go. It makes her feel very sad. She is getting older now. At 64 years old, she wants to go on this special trip before she gets too tired. Her dream feels like it might slip away. Did you know? Many people in the world hope to go on a special trip one day. It could be to visit family, see a new animal, or climb a mountain! This waiting makes Najia feel stuck. She wants to fulfill her religious duty. She wants to pray at the holy sites. She wants to share this special time with her husband. But the "blockade" makes it almost impossible. It's like having your bags packed but the door won't open.

A Call for Kindness and Hope

Najia's story shows us something important. It shows us that everyone has dreams. And everyone deserves a chance to follow their dreams. It makes us think about people who find it hard to travel. It reminds us to be kind and understand that some people face big challenges. Did you know? Many organizations around the world work to help people travel safely and fairly. They believe everyone should have the freedom to move. We hope that Najia and her husband will get their chance. We hope the doors will open for them. We hope they can finally make their special journey to Mecca. It would be a wonderful day for them. And it would be a happy day for everyone who believes in kindness and making dreams come true. Let's send our happy thoughts to Najia! Maybe one day, she will be able to share her amazing Hajj story with us.
summary: A kind grandma named Najia in Gaza dreams of going on a special holy trip called Hajj with her husband, but strict rules make it very hard for her to leave her home.
whyMatters: This story helps us understand that everyone has important dreams, and it shows us how difficult it can be for some people to travel, making us wish for kindness and freedom for all.
